revision.text:
[[File:Amun-Ra.jpg|alt=Amun-Re, Karnak Temple [de; ja; draft]|thumb]]
'''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.
=== Special Features === The sun god Ra can also become the god of the dead Osiris and merge with him. This is made possible by the fact that Ra dies on his nocturnal journey through the [[:en:Amduat|Underworld]] and is reborn as [[:en:Khepri|Chepre]] (also: Chepri) in the morning.
== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.
== References == <references />

=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:

Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q172904]] Wikidata name: syncretism [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Amun-Ra.jpg|alt=Amun-Re, Karnak Temple [de; ja; draft]|thumb]]
'''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.
=== Special Features === The sun god Ra can also become the god of the dead Osiris and merge with him. This is made possible by the fact that Ra dies on his nocturnal journey through the [[:en:Amduat|Underworld]] and is reborn as [[:en:Khepri|Chepre]] (also: Chepri) in the morning.
== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.
== References == <references />

''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. '''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.
=== Special Features === The sun god Ra can also become the god of the dead Osiris and merge with him. This is made possible by the fact that Ra dies on his nocturnal journey through the [[:en:Amduat|Underworld]] and is reborn as [[:en:Khepri|Chepre]] (also: Chepri) in the morning.
== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.

== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)

=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.

Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>

'''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.
=== Special Features === The sun god Ra can also become the god of the dead Osiris and merge with him. This is made possible by the fact that Ra dies on his nocturnal journey through the [[:en:Amduat|Underworld]] and is reborn as [[:en:Khepri|Chepre]] (also: Chepri) in the morning.
== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.

== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.

Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>

'''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.
=== Special Features === The sun god Ra can also become the god of the dead Osiris and merge with him. This is made possible by the fact that Ra dies on his nocturnal journey through the [[:en:Amduat|Underworld]] and is reborn as [[:en:Khepri|Chepre]] (also: Chepri) in the morning.
== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.

'''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. == Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.

Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>

'''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.
=== Special Features === The sun god Ra can also become the god of the dead Osiris and merge with him. This is made possible by the fact that Ra dies on his nocturnal journey through the [[:en:Amduat|Underworld]] and is reborn as [[:en:Khepri|Chepre]] (also: Chepri) in the morning.
== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.

'''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.

== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' '''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.
=== Special Features === The sun god Ra can also become the god of the dead Osiris and merge with him. This is made possible by the fact that Ra dies on his nocturnal journey through the [[:en:Amduat|Underworld]] and is reborn as [[:en:Khepri|Chepre]] (also: Chepri) in the morning.
== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.

'''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.
=== Special Features === The sun god Ra can also become the god of the dead Osiris and merge with him. This is made possible by the fact that Ra dies on his nocturnal journey through the [[:en:Amduat|Underworld]] and is reborn as [[:en:Khepri|Chepre]] (also: Chepri) in the morning.
== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.

The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. '''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.
=== Special Features === The sun god Ra can also become the god of the dead Osiris and merge with him. This is made possible by the fact that Ra dies on his nocturnal journey through the [[:en:Amduat|Underworld]] and is reborn as [[:en:Khepri|Chepre]] (also: Chepri) in the morning.
== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.
== References == <references />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ab:Асинкретизм]][[af:Sinkretisme]][[ar:توفيق بين الأديان]][[arz:توفيق بين الاديان]][[ast:Sincretismu]][[az:Sinkretizm]][[be:Сінкрэтызм]][[bg:Синкретизъм]][[bn:সমন্বয়বাদ]][[ca:Sincretisme]][[cs:Synkretismus]][[da:Synkretisme]][[de:Synkretismus]][[el:Συγκρητισμός]][[en:Syncretism]][[eo:Sinkretismo]][[es:Sincretismo]][[et:Sünkretism]][[eu:Sinkretismo]][[fa:تلفیق‌گرایی]][[fi:Synkretismi]][[fr:Syncrétisme]][[fy:Synkretisme]][[gl:Sincretismo]][[he:סינקרטיזם]][[hr:Sinkretizam]][[hu:Szinkretizmus]][[hy:Սինկրետիզմ]][[ia:Syncretismo]][[id:Sinkretisme]][[io:Sinkretismo]][[it:Sincretismo]][[ja:シンクレティズム]][[ka:სინკრეტიზმი]][[kk:Синкретизм]][[ko:혼합주의]][[ky:Синкретизм]][[la:Syncretismus]][[lt:Sinkretizmas]][[lv:Sinkrētisms]][[mg:Sinkretisma]][[ml:വിശ്വാസ സമന്വയം]][[ms:Sinkretisme]][[nl:Syncretisme]][[nn:Synkretisme]][[no:Synkretisme]][[oc:Sincretisme]][[pl:Synkretyzm]][[pt:Sincretismo]][[ro:Sincretism]][[ru:Синкретизм (философия)]][[sh:Sinkretizam]][[simple:Syncretism]][[sk:Synkretizmus]][[sl:Sinkretizem]][[sr:Синкретизам]][[sv:Synkretism]][[sw:Sinkretisimo]][[th:การผสานความเชื่อ]][[tl:Sinkretismo]][[tr:Senkretizm]][[uk:Синкретизм]][[ur:امتزاج ضدین]][[uz:Sinkretizm]][[vi:Tính nguyên hợp]][[war:Sinkretismo]][[wuu:综摄]][[xmf:რელიგიური სინკრეტიზმი]][[zh-yue:混合主義]][[zh:折衷论]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q172904]] Wikidata name: syncretism [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Amun-Ra.jpg|alt=Amun-Re, Karnak Temple [de; ja; draft]|thumb]]
'''Synkretismus''' means in [[:en:Egyptian Mythology|Egyptian Mythology]] that two or more deities merge into a unified whole, as for example Atum-Ra or {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・レ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whereby the pan-regional [[:en:Solar deity|Sun God]] [[:en:Ra|Ra]] connects with the creation god [[:en:Atum|Atum]] of [[:en:Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]] or with the Theban [[:en:Amun|Amun]] (the Hidden) of [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], thereby forming a new deity that can stand alongside the two original gods. Thus, Atum–Ra designates the aspect of the evening sun god, while Ra generally refers to the sun god.
== Prerequisites == The potential for the deities to merge is fundamentally linked to the three-thousand-year history of [[:en:Ancient Egyptian religion|Religious History]], the transformation of the divine image, the emergence of new deities such as [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] from the end of the 4th Dynasty, the Theban Amun from the end of the [[:en:First Intermediate Period of Egypt|First Intermediate Period]], and the adoption of Asian deities in the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|Neuen Reich]]. Additionally, there is no theological codification, so the characteristics of the deities remain fluid. This is also reflected in their [[:en:Iconography|Iconography]].
The possible equivalence of the deities depends on their ''gender'', their ''attributes'' and ''functions''. Some, initially only local deities, are elevated by merging with a pan-regional god before, like Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom, becoming an overwhelmingly powerful deity.
== Examples of Deity Syncretism == === Old Kingdom === * [[:en:Ptah|Ptah]]-[[:en:Sokar|Sokar]] (merging of the creator god of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] with the necropolis god of Memphis)
=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
=== Late Period === Only in the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|エジプトの後期|lt=Late Period|lt_de=Spätzeit|lt_ja=エジプトの後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} do mergers of male and female deities also occur. In the [[:en:Ptolemaic dynasty|Ptolemaic]] period, [[:en:Serapis|Serapis]] (Osiris–Apis) is added as a new syncretic deity, whereby Osiris forms a connection with [[:en:Apis (deity)|Apis]], the Memphite bull deity, adopts [[:en:Dionysus|dionysian]] traits, and also gains entry into the Roman pantheon.
=== Special Features === The sun god Ra can also become the god of the dead Osiris and merge with him. This is made possible by the fact that Ra dies on his nocturnal journey through the [[:en:Amduat|Underworld]] and is reborn as [[:en:Khepri|Chepre]] (also: Chepri) in the morning.
== Literature == * [[:en:Jan Assmann|Jan Assmann]]: ''Ra und Amun. Die Krise des polytheistischen Weltbilds im Ägypten der 18.-20. Dynastie'' (= ''Biblicus et Orientalis.'' Band 51). Universitätsverlag/ Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Fribourg/ Göttingen 1983, ISBN 3-7278-0278-2. * Jan Assmann: ''Ägypten. Theologie und Frömmigkeit einer frühen Hochkultur'' (= ''Urban-Taschenbücher.'' Band 366). Kohlhammer, Stuttgart et al. 1984, ISBN 3-17-008371-6. * [[:en:Erik Hornung|Erik Hornung]]: ''Der Eine und die Vielen: altägyptische Götterwelt''. 6th, completely revised and expanded edition, Primus-Verlag, Darmstadt 2005, ISBN 3-89678-539-7. * {{ill|Wolfgang Schenkel|de|Wolfgang Schenkel|ja|ヴォルフガング・シェンケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Schenkel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in: Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume II: Stichwort ''Götterverschmelzung''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1977, columns 720–725.
== References == <references />

=== Middle Kingdom === * Amun-Ra from the 12th Dynasty * [[:en:Sobek|Sobek]]-Ra from the 12th Dynasty – merging and elevation of the crocodile-formed principal deity of the [[:en:Faiyum|Fayum]] with the pan-regional sun god Ra * [[:en:Khnum|Chnum]]-Ra (Chnum as originally a local deity from [[:en:Esna|Esna]] and in the area of the 1st cataract) * Ptah–Sokar-Osiris (merging of three deities, see Ptah-Sokar)
=== New Kingdom === [[File:Osiris-Apis_MGEg_Inv22847-9.jpg|alt=Antinoos [de; ja; draft] as Serapis|thumb]]
The New Kingdom is characterized by expansion into the Near East and the adoption of [[:en:Canaan|Canaan]]ite deities. Thus, in the [[:en:Great Sphinx of Giza|Great Sphinx]] of [[:en:Giza|Giza]], the sun god Ra is venerated in his manifestation as {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Horus on the Horizon), while simultaneously also venerating the Canaanite god {{ill|Hauron (God)|de|Hauron (Gott)|ja|ハウロン|lt=Hauron|lt_de=Hauron|lt_ja=ハウロン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hauron (God)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, resulting in a merging known as Hauron-Harmachis.
In the New Kingdom, from [[:en:Tutankhamun|Tutankhamun]] onwards, who relocated from [[:en:Amarna|Amarna]], not to [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]], but to [[:en:Memphis|Memphis]], there is initially a politically motivated equation of Amun–Ra-Ptah, who is now regarded as the {{ill|World God|de|Weltgott|ja|世界神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World God|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>Jan Assmann: ''Ägyptische Hymnen und Gebete. Eingeleitet, übersetzt und erläutert'' (= ''Bibliothek der alten Welt.''). Artemis, Zürich 1975.</ref> The significance of the trinity of Amun, Ra, and Ptah is also evident from the {{ill|Amun Hymn of Leiden|de|Leidener Amunhymnus|ja|ライデンのアメン讃歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun Hymn of Leiden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}:
''Drei sind alle Götter: Amun, Ra und Ptah, keinem gibt es ihresgleichen. Verborgen ist sein Name als Amun, als Ra wird er wahrgenommen, sein Leib ist Ptah. Ihre Städte auf Erden bleiben immerdar: Thebes, Heliopolis und Memphis, bis ans Ende der Zeit.''<ref>Zitiert nach Erik Hornung: ''Der Eine und die Vielen. Ägyptische Gottesvorstellungen.'' 1. Auflage, Wissenschaftliche Buchgesellschaft, Darmstadt 1971, S. 215.</ref>
